coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive turn-off fluorescence detection of iodide using carbon dots/gold nanocluster as fluorescent nanoprobe
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Xue
Hu, Jie
Zhu, Huanhuan
Chen, Qiumeng
Gong, Zhengjun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel fluorescence turn-off sensing strategy for I− ions detection was developed. The fluorescence of CDs/His-AuNCs hybrid probes was effectively turned off by I− ions. It was attributed to the internal filtration effect between the fluorescence emission peak of CDs and the absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs enhanced by I− ions. Quantitative analysis of I− ions can be achieved by analyzing the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs/His-AuNCs. Under the optimal experimental conditions, it exhibited good linearity in two concentration ranges of I− ions: 0.1–5.0 μM and 5.0–60.0 μM (low detection limit = 34 nM). The established sensing platform has been successfully exploited for the detection of I− ions in practical water samples. This work provided new insight into the determination of anions using fluorescent nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoclusters
Internal filtration effect
Fluorescence quenching
Iodide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection I− ions using CDs/His-AuNCs
The possible sensing mechanism for I− ions assay
Analytical performance for I− ions detection
I− ions detection in practical water samples

We appreciate the assistance with the experimental conditions provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and size distribution histograms of CDs (a, c) and His-AuNCs (b, d).
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, His-AuNCs and CDs/His-AuNCs; (b) The whole XPS spectra of CDs; (c) Au 4f spectra of CDs; (d) The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs/His-AuNCs.

(a) The emission spectrum of CDs, His-AuNCs, and CDs/His-AuNCs before and after adding I− ions, the inset was an enlarged view of His-AuNCs; (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of I−, His-AuNCs and His-AuNCs + I−; (c) the UV–vis absorption spectra of His-AuNCs with a series of concentrations of I− ions; (d) fluorescence decay curve of CDs before and after addition of His-AuNCs and/or I− ions.
Effects of (a) volume ratio of His-AuNCs to CDs, (b) pH value, and (c) reaction time on quenching effect of CDs/His-AuNCs system.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs/His-AuNCs at various I− ions concentrations; (b) Linear calibration chart of CDs/His-AuNCs for I− ions detection; (c-d) Anti interference experiment of detection of I− ions by CDs/His-AuNCs.
Comparison of the analytical properties for iodide with some previously reported methods.
Results of I− ions determination in some samples using the CDs/His-AuNCs fluorescent probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive turn-off fluorescence detection of iodide using carbon dots/gold nanocluster as fluorescent nanoprobe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 611756, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu 611756, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State-Province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State-Province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu, Sichuan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, Sichuan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence turn-off sensing strategy for I− ions detection was developed based on the internal filtering effect between CDs and iodide-induced aggregation His-AuNCs.

In this work, a novel fluorescence turn-off sensing strategy for I− ions detection was developed. The fluorescence of CDs/His-AuNCs hybrid probes was effectively turned off by I− ions. It was attributed to the internal filtration effect between the fluorescence emission peak of CDs and the absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs enhanced by I− ions. Quantitative analysis of I− ions can be achieved by analyzing the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs/His-AuNCs. Under the optimal experimental conditions, it exhibited good linearity in two concentration ranges of I− ions: 0.1–5.0 μM and 5.0–60.0 μM (low detection limit = 34 nM). The established sensing platform has been successfully exploited for the detection of I− ions in practical water samples. This work provided new insight into the determination of anions using fluorescent nanoparticles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The specific detection of inorganic anions is of utmost significance because anions are ubiquitous in nature and play a vital role in a range of biological processes [1–4]. Iodide (I−) has attracted much attention due to its important effects on some physiological processes, including basic metabolism and intellectual development [5]. Insufficient or excessive iodide intake can cause serious health issues, such as cretinism, goiter, nervous system diseases and neoplastic diseases [6–10]. Iodine is a vital raw element that only comes from exogenous sources (such as drinking water and foods) because the body cannot synthesize it endogenously [11]. In different regions, the iodine content in drinking water varies greatly, which is mainly affected by many factors, such as proximity to seawater, soil iodine content, and agricultural run-off [9]. For example, health problems caused by insufficient iodine intake are more common in areas where iodine is deficient in farm soil (e.g. the Himalayan, Atlas, Alps, and Andes ranges) and drinking water (e.g. Israel using desalinated water) [6]. Therefore, the specific and ultrasensitive detection of iodide in environmental samples is of great significance.

To date, a number of analytical techniques have been presented for iodide detection, including ion chromatography, electrochemical technology, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry, optical spectroscopy, and so on [12–18]. Among these methods, fluorescent sensors have been widely employed for the detection and tracking of diverse analytes with its specificity, high sensitivity, simplicity, and low cost. Carbon dots (CDs) show outstanding optical properties, high photostability, low toxicity and biocompatibility, which make them widely studied in the sensing field [19–22]. However, most of the developed carbon dots are negatively charged and difficult to use as anion sensors [23]. Currently, some CDs-based fluorescence sensors for iodide detection have been reported. Most of these detect iodide indirectly by removing M n
+ from the CDs/M n
+ system and restoring the fluorescence of CDs. This phenomenon could be caused by the strong and specific interaction between M n
+ (e.g., Hg2+, Ag+, and Cu2+) and iodide [24–27]. Therefore, great efforts on exploring the application of carbon dots in anion sensing are still required.

Herein, a novel fluorescent sensing probe for I− ions was developed by simply mixing CDs and histidine-stabilized gold nanoclusters (His-AuNCs). First, I− ions were adsorbed onto His-AuNCs through electrostatic interaction to induce the fusion and aggregation of His-AuNCs. Second, the presence of I− ions enhanced the absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs and caused a new absorption peak at 550 nm. Finally, the enhanced absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs overlapped with the emission peak of the CDs. The fluorescence of the CDs is turned off due to internal filtration effects (IFE). The established method was successfully used for evaluating environmental water samples and provided a new strategy for ultrasensitive I− ions detection.

Citric acid (CA) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. l-histidine was bought from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Anhui, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Potassium iodide (KI) was purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. A certified environmental standard sample, i.e., BY400173 with I− content of 5.19 ± 0.32 mg L−1, was obtained from Tanmo Quality Inspection Technology Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Other substances were supplied by the Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All reagents and chemicals are analytically grade and used straight away. All the solutions in this experiment were prepared with ultra-pure water.

Fluorescence and fluorescence lifespan were measured using the FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). UV–visible measurements were performed on a USB-4000 portable UV–vis spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA). A PHS-4C+ pH meter (Fangzhou Technology, China) was used to measure the buffer solutions’ pH. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken with a Tecnai G2 F20 high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, America). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was acquired by a Spectrum 2 instrument (PerkinElmer, USA) in transmission mode. The XPS spectra of CDs and His-AuNCs were collected using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The surface charge of the probe was determined using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UA).

The previously reported procedure was applied to synthesize CDs and His-AuNCs with minor modifications [28,29]. The hydrothermal process was employed to produce CDs as follows. 20 mL of water were employed to dissolve citric acid (CA, 1 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 1042 µL). The above solution was then placed into a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) and heated for 9 h at 180 ℃. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled to ambient temperature spontaneously to obtain CDs.

His-AuNCs were produced with l-histidine as a reducing agent and protective ligand. Briefly, HAuCl4 (10 mM, 1.0 mL) and l-histidine (3 mL, 0.1 M) were mixed in a small vial and agitated for 2 h at ambient temperature. The obtained CDs and His-AuNCs were kept in storage at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection I− ions using CDs/His-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical I− ions detection experiment, the CDs/His-AuNCs probe was facilely prepared by simply mixing His-AuNCs with 150 times diluted solution of CDs according to a certain volume ratio (1:0.25, 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4). Next, 500 μL of CDs/His-AuNCs probe, 50 μL I− ions solution with a known concentration, and 900 μL 10 mM phosphate buffer (PBS, pH = 4) were mixed at constant temperature temperature. After a while, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution was determined, where the excitation wavelength was 347 nm (0.5 nm slit), and the emission wavelength was 447 nm (0.2 nm slit). Under favorable experimental conditions, a series of I− ions solution (0–100 μM) mixed with the CDs/His-AuNCs fluorescent probe, and the associated emission intensities at 447 nm were measured. The anti-interference ability of CDs/His-AuNCs was evaluated using a variety of common anions and cations. All experiments were conducted three times each.

To prove the successful synthesis of CDs and His-AuNCs, transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and zeta potential were utilized to investigate their nanostructure and surface properties. The particle size and morphology of CDs and His-AuNCs were studied using TEM. As depicted in Fig. 1 c, the prepared CDs were nearly spherical (mean diameter of 6.86 nm) and had good dispersion. The HRTEM images for individual CDs revealed well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice constant of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1a), matching the graphite carbon (100) lattice plane [30]. In the formation of His-AuNCs, Au3+ was reduced to Au(0) by the imidazole moiety of l-histidine and stabilized by l-histidine adsorbed on its surface. As shown in Fig. 1d, His-AuNCs were spherical (mean diameter of 3.36 nm) and had good dispersion. The HRTEM image showed that the lattice distance is 0.24 nm (inset, Fig. 1b), in agreement with the face-centered cubic Au (111) lattice plane [31].

The surface properties of CDs and His-AuNCs were evaluated using FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peaks at 3420 cm−1 with broadband, 2923 cm−1 (2854 cm−1), and 1389 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of NH/OH, CH, and CN, respectively. Moreover, the vibrational absorption band of CO and the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CN/HC were presented at 1600 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, respectively. These peaks lined up with those that have been documented in the literature [28]. The FTIR characteristic absorption of His-AuNCs was basically consistent with that of l-histidine, since the surface of the Au core was covered by l-histidine [29,32]. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2b) exhibited three diffraction peaks at 285.75, 400.59, and 530.79 eV, which could be attributed to C 1 s (44.44 %), N 1 s (18.4 %) and O 1 s (37.16 %), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the high-resolution C 1 s peak could be fitted four peaks at 284.4, 285.3, 286.1 and 287.7 eV, in agreement with CC, CN, CO, and CO/CN, respectively. The high resolution O 1 s revealed two peaks at 530.3 and 531.6 eV assigned to CO and CO groups. In addition, the fitting of the high-resolution N1s spectrum showed that there were two types of nitrogen bonds: pyrrolic N (400.7 eV) and pyridinic N (399.4 eV). The above characterizations were consistent with the FT-IR analysis, which indicated that the obtained CDs have abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups and carboxyl groups. The binding energy of Au 4f7/2 was located at 84.3 eV (Fig. 2c), indicating that the product is a gold cluster composed of Au0 atoms [29]. Additionally, the presence of C, O, and N components derived from amino acid molecules in His-AuNCs (Fig. S1d) demonstrated that the atomic gold cluster was stabilized by l-histidine.

Fig. 2d and Fig. S2 images illustrated the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, His-AuNCs, and CDs/His-AuNCs, respectively. The results in Fig. S2 showed that two absorption peaks at 238 and 347 nm were visible in the UV–vis spectra of CDs, ascribed as the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and the n-π* transitions of CO bonds, respectively. Significantly, the excitation peak at 347 nm of the CDs was consistent with its absorption band (280–400 nm), suggesting that the emission spectrum should be associated with the surface CO relevant defects [33]. His-AuNCs displayed an absorption band in UV–vis spectra between 240 and 450 nm, with a small peak around 255 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission peak of His-AuNCs is 494 nm under the excitation wavelength at 381 nm, which is consistent with the previously reported gold nanoclusters [29]. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs/His-AuNCs hybrid probe was mainly consistent with that of CDs, which also exhibited a good absorption at 347 nm (Fig. 2d). As a result, under stimulation at 347 nm, CDs/His-AuNCs exhibited an emission peak at 447 nm, which largely accords with the emission of the bare CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible sensing mechanism for I− ions assay
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the fluorescent probe consisted of a simple mixture of two different fluorescent nanoparticles of both CDs and His-AuNCs. The determination of I− was carried out by the fluorescence turn off process based on the CDs/His-AuNCs sensing probes. To investigate the application potential of this CDs/His-AuNCs probe, the fluorescence interaction between the probe primitive unit CDs and His-AuNCs should be discussed. As shown in Fig. S2, the CDs (His-AuNCs) emission peak and the His-AuNCs (CDs) UV–vis absorption band displayed no apparent spectral overlap, which indicates no energy transfer between the two nanomaterials [34]. Furthermore, the mean fluorescence lifetime of CDs before and after mixing with His-AuNCs was virtually maintained, which were 14.65 and 14.46 ns, respectively (Fig. 3 d). Therefore, CDs/His-AuNCs can be employed as fluorescent probes to detect I− ions without interference with one other.

The fluorescence spectrum, UV–vis spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime were measured to investigate the fluorescence quenching process of CDs/His-AuNCs. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the intense fluorescence of CDs did not change significantly with the addition of I− ions, while the fluorescence emission of His-AuNCs was quenched to a certain extent. In contrast, the CDs/His-AuNCs hybrid probes not only have strong fluorescence emission, but also can be quenched with high efficiency after adding of I− ions. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of CDs and His-AuNCs did not effectively overlap with the UV–vis spectra of I− ions (Fig. 3b). These phenomena indicated no interaction between CDs and I− ions. However, it is worth noting that, with the addition of I− ions, the absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs was enhanced, and a new wide absorption band (∼550 nm) appeared (Fig. 3b). It indicated the formation of ground-state complexes between His-AuNC and I− ions. The fluorescence lifetime of His-AuNCs remained almost unchanged after the addition of iodine ions, 5.32 ns and 5.46 ns, respectively. It suggested that the quenching mechanism of His-AuNCs is static quenching. Specifically, I− ions are adsorbed on positively charged His-AuNCs through electrostatic interaction (Fig. S3), which increases van der Waals gravity between His-AuNCs to fuse and aggregate [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence emission peak of CDs overlapped with the absorption band of iodide-enhanced His-Au NCs. Besides, as the concentration of I− ions increases, the absorption spectrum of His-AuNCs gradually increases (Fig. 3c), and the degree of overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of CDs also increases. The quenching mechanism of CDs fluorescence may be the internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To elucidate the potential quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of the associated probes before and after the addition of I− ions were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 3d that the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs/His-AuNCs hardly changed in the presence of I− ions. Therefore, the mechanism of CDs being quenched by His-AuNCs-I− was explained by the inner filter effect. In brief, the sensing of I− ions was based on the internal filtering effect between CDs and the aggregated His-AuNCs induced by iodide, which efficiently turned off the fluorescence of CDs.

To obtain better detection of I− ions, some important detection conditions were optimized, including the volume ratio of His-AuNCs to diluted 150-fold CDs, pH value, and reaction time. The volume ratio of His-AuNCs to diluted 150-fold CDs is closely associated with the sensitivity of fluorescence measurement. When His-AuNCs and CDs were mixed in different volume ratios (1:0.25, 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4), the emission intensity of CDs/His-AuNCs and the quenching efficiency after adding I− ions were systematically studied. As the CDs volume grew, the emission intensity of CDs/His-AuNCs probe rose steadily, while the fluorescence quenching strength caused by I− ions decreases (Fig. 4 a). The information above demonstrated that the fluorescence signal of CDs/His-AuNCs probe mainly comes from CDs, and the optimal volume ratio of His-AuNCs/CDs is 1:2.

The influence of pH on the CDs/His-AuNCs-I− system was investigated in a wide pH range (2–13). As depicted in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence emission of CDs/His-AuNCs was strong and held steady in the wide pH range of 3 to 12, but weakened in extremely acidic or alkaline conditions. This phenomenon may be caused by strong influence on the surface or molecular state of the probe under extreme pH conditions [28]. However, when I− ions was added into the CDs/His-AuNCs sensing system, the fluorescent intensity of CDs/His-AuNCs was quenched by I−, and the quenching effect was gradually decreased with the pH increasing from 3 to 13. In the pH range of 3.0–4.0, iodide showed good quenching efficiency for CDs/His-AuNCs. pH 4.0 was applied in the following experiments. Besides, after adding I− ions for 5 min, the quenching efficiency reached the maximum and remained almost unchanged upon increasing the reaction time (Fig. 4c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for I− ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of CDs/His-AuNCs for I− ions detection was evaluated. As the iodide concentration increased from 0 to 100 μM, the fluorescence emission of CDs/His-AuNCs at 447 nm was decreased (Fig. 5 a). The calibration graph of the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0/F-1) versus I− ions concentration was linear within 0.1–5 and 5.0–60 μM (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence intensities of CDs/His-AuNCs in the presence or absence of I− ions were labeled as F and F 0, respectively. The detection limit (LOD, 3σ) for I− ions was 34 nM. The analytical performance of this method was compared with other reported methods based on CDs or AuNCs (Au NPs) for iodide detection. It can be observed that the present technology has a wide linear range and a lower detection limit (Table 1 ).

Considering the application prospect of CDs/His-AuNCs sensor system in the environmental field, the anti-interference ability of the sensor system for detecting I− ions were studied. In the presence of some prevalent anions and cations, such as SCN−, CH3COO−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, S2−, IO3 −, Br−, Cl−, F−, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Al3+, the fluorescence response of the CDs/His-AuNCs towards I− ions was recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5c-d, the influence of these anions and cations on the sensing signal of I− ions can be ignored, proving that the proposed sensing system has excellent anti-interference towards I− ions. Therefore, CDs/His-AuNCs can be used as a highly selective fluorescent sensing system for I− ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: I− ions detection in practical water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Given its excellent analytical performance, the feasibility of the proposed analytical method was investigated by detecting the concentration of I− ions in actual water samples and standard samples. The samples of lake and tap water were gathered and filtered via 0.22 μm membranes, and then analyzed by spiked recovery method. The analysis results were listed in Table 2 . For standard samples, the acquired I− ions contents were basically consistent with the certified values. The spiked recovery rate of I− ions in the actual water sample was between 94 % and 106 %. The above results showed that the CDs/His-AuNCs fluorescent probe has practical application for the detection of I− ions.

In conclusion, we have synthesized the CDs/His-AuNCs with excellent fluorescence properties, which can be served as a fluorescent probe to detect I− ions selectively and sensitively. Through internal filtration effects, the fluorescence emission of CDs was turned off by the aggregated His-AuNCs induced via iodide. The concentration of I− ions could be measured by the fluorescence intensity quenching degree of CDs/His-AuNCs system. The linear range for I− ions was 0.1–5.0 μM and 5.0–60.0 μM (LOD = 34 nM). Additionally, such CDs/His-AuNCs fluorescent probe has been further utilized to detect I− ions in environment samples. This study provided a novel strategy for developing CDs-based fluorescence methods to detect other anions.

Xue Zou: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Jie Hu: Methodology, Investigation. Huanhuan Zhu: Resources, Investigation. Qiumeng Chen: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhengjun Gong: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176154), and Science and Technology Department Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022YFS0502, 2022NSFSC0989 & 2022024). We appreciate the assistance with the experimental conditions provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108275.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric and fluorescent anion sensors: an overview of recent developments in the use of 1,8-naphthalimide-based chemosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Duke, E.B. Veale, F.M. Pfeffer, P.E. Kruger, T. Gunnlaugsson, Colorimetric and fluorescent anion sensors: an overview of recent developments in the use of 1,8-naphthalimide-based chemosensors, Chem. Soc. Rev., 39 (2010) 3936-3953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-cyclic formylated dipyrromethanes as phosphate anion receptors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Deliomeroglu, V.M. Lynch, J.L. Sessler, Non-cyclic formylated dipyrromethanes as phosphate anion receptors, Chem. Sci., 7 (2016) 3843-3850.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Naphthalene-glycine conjugate: An extremely selective colorimetric chemosensor for iodide ion in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Thakur, S.R. Bhatta, B. Mondal, D. Kakash, P. Chawla, Naphthalene-glycine conjugate: An extremely selective colorimetric chemosensor for iodide ion in aqueous solution, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem., 267 (2018) 617-626.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An array of metallic nanozymes can discriminate and detect a large number of anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sharifi, J. Tashkhourian, B. Hemmateenejad, An array of metallic nanozymes can discriminate and detect a large number of anions, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem., 339 (2021) 129911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reappraisal of the risk of iodine-induced hyperthyroidism: An epidemiological population survey

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Azizi, M. Hedayati, M. Rahmani, R. Sheikholeslam, S. Allahverdian, N. Salarkia, Reappraisal of the risk of iodine-induced hyperthyroidism: An epidemiological population survey, J Endocrinol. Invest., 28 (2005) 23-29.
M.B. Zimmermann, Iodine deficiency, Endocr. Rev., 30 (2009) 376-408.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thyroid function among breastfed children with chronically excessive iodine intakes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Aakre, T.A. Strand, T. Bjoro, I. Norheim, I. Barikmo, S. Ares, M.D. Alcorta, S. Henjum, Thyroid function among breastfed children with chronically excessive iodine intakes, Nutrients, 8 (2016) 398.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of iodine for thyroid function in lactating women and infants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Andersson, C.P. Braegger, The role of iodine for thyroid function in lactating women and infants, Endocr. Rev., (2021) 1-38.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iodine: its role in thyroid hormone biosynthesis and beyond

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sorrenti, E. Baldini, D. Pironi, A. Lauro, V. D'Orazi, F. Tartaglia, D. Tripodi, E. Lori, F. Gagliardi, M. Pratico, G. Illuminati, V. D'Andrea, P. Palumbo, S. Ulisse, Iodine: its role in thyroid hormone biosynthesis and beyond, Nutrients, 13 (2021) 4469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of iodide and mercuric ions by nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on flow injection “turn off-on” chemiluminescence analysis system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, Y. Li, S. Han, Simultaneous detection of iodide and mercuric ions by nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots based on flow injection “turn off-on” chemiluminescence analysis system, Microchem. J., 147 (2019) 1141-1146.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, S. Han, Carbon dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide, Microchem. J., 154 (2020) 104638.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent/colorimetric probes and (chemo-) sensors for detecting anions based on transition and lanthanide ion receptor/binding complexes
